questions.question: Why use a trie instead of a hash table for string keys?
options: Prefix queries: 'list all keys starting with X' in O(|X| + |result|). Hash tables can't do this — they'd need to scan every bucket. Used for autocomplete, IP route LPM, JSON path queries.
questions.explanation: A hash table maps key → value with O(1) lookup but loses all structure. A trie indexes character-by-character along the tree path — so 'list all words with prefix "car"' is: walk to the 'car' node, do a subtree traversal. Auto-complete in your editor, search-as-you-type, IP routing all need this. Hash table can't be used because keys are spread over all buckets.

questions.question: A radix trie (Patricia trie) differs from a plain trie how?
options: Single-child chains are COMPRESSED into a single edge labeled with a string. Same set of keys, far fewer nodes (~½ for typical dictionaries).
questions.explanation: Plain trie storing 'card': root → c → a → r → d → terminal — 4 internal nodes. Radix trie: root → 'card' → terminal — 1 node. The compression saves O(m) memory where m is total characters. Lookup still O(|word|). Linux's IP routing table uses a binary radix trie on 32-bit addresses; without compression it would need 4 billion nodes.

questions.question: What is 'longest prefix match' (LPM) and why is it important for IP routing?
options: Given a destination IP, find the most-specific route in the routing table that matches some prefix of the IP. Used to pick the correct outgoing interface. Done in radix trees in O(32) for IPv4.
questions.explanation: Internet routing has overlapping prefixes: 10.0.0.0/8 → route A; 10.5.0.0/16 → route B. For destination 10.5.1.1, we want route B (more specific). LPM finds the deepest matching node in the radix trie. Linux does this for every packet's destination IP — 100M+ times per second on a busy router. With 800K BGP routes, an LPC trie keeps lookup to ~30 cycles.

questions.question: What's the memory cost of a naïve trie for a dictionary of 100K English words?
options: ~1M nodes × 26 pointers each = ~200 MB on 64-bit. Mostly empty slots. Compressed (radix or hash-per-node) brings this to ~10-20 MB.
questions.explanation: Total characters in 100K English words is ~700K. Plain trie node has 26 pointers × 8 bytes = 208 bytes; ~700K nodes → 145 MB. Most slots are empty (English words use ~10 different starting letters densely). Radix tree compression cuts node count to ~50K → 10 MB. For static dictionaries, a DAWG can compress further by merging equivalent suffixes.

questions.question: Why does Linux use radix trees so much in its kernel (page cache, NUMA, IRQ descriptors)?
options: Sparse integer-keyed maps. Page cache: file offset → page pointer; many billions of possible offsets, only thousands used per file. Radix tree gives O(log n) lookup with O(used) memory — much better than a hash table for sparse data.
questions.explanation: Hash tables size memory by capacity (a 4GB-cap table costs 4GB even if empty); radix trees grow only with the data actually present. For Linux's page cache (sparse mapping from 64-bit file offsets to page pointers), a radix tree uses memory proportional to used pages, not file size. Lookup is O(log n) with very small constants — usually O(6) for typical usage. Linus called the radix tree 'one of the few non-trivial data structures we got right'.

questions.question: How do tries enable fuzzy search (e.g., 'find words within edit distance 2 of "banaa"')?
options: Walk the trie + a DP edit-distance table in parallel. Prune subtrees where the minimum cost in the column exceeds the threshold. Avoids most of the work of naive O(n × |word|²) per-word comparison.
questions.explanation: Naïve fuzzy search compares the query against every word — O(N × |query| × |word|). Trie + DP: maintain a Levenshtein DP row per trie node; the row is computed incrementally as you descend. If the row's minimum exceeds the threshold k, prune the whole subtree. Used in spell-checkers, search-as-you-type, fuzzy IDE search. Hunspell, Lucene fuzzy queries — all variations on this.
